text: This video shows robotic three-stage esophagectomy for a T-IV esophageal cancer with da Vinci X system. Performed by Dr. Somshaker SP, HOD, robotic surgeon and hypox specialist, Manipal Comprehensive Cancer Center, Manipal Hospital, Bangalore.
A six-year male patient with a good performance status was evaluated for dysphagia for liquids and solids for one month.
On upper GI endoscopy, a fungating mass was noted between thirty-two to forty centimeters of esophagus, biopsy of which revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, grade two.

CT scan thorax was done, which showed a marked irregular thickening of lower esophagus, mass lesion in esophagus in close relation to left atrium and pulmonary veins, posteriorly descending iota and left posteriorly.
Prominent subcarnal lymph nodes were also noted. CT scan thorax showing the presence of disease very close to atrium and pulmonary vein posteriorly.
Metastatic workup was done for the patient, which revealed no distant metastasis. Patient was staged to be T-for-N-one-M-zero disease.

He received four cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, float regimen. Post neoadjuvant chemotherapy scan showed good response, but There was persistent mass lesion in close relation to atrium and pulmonary vein and descending aorta. He was worked up for surgery.

Plan was to do robotic transthoracic and transperitoneal three-stage esophagectomy with two-field lymphadenectomy with gastric pull-up and cervical anastomosis. Patient is placed in prone position with arm being hyper abducted. is adequately padded and strapped.
The surgeon marks the tip of the scapula on the patient's back as part of the port placement process for the esophagectomy procedure.

The surgeon places the camera port one intercostal space below the tip of the scapula, with the midline of the spine marked for reference. An additional arm port is positioned seven centimeters away from the midline, two intercostal spaces above the camera port.

The surgeon places arm two seven centimeters away from the midline, positioned two or three intercostal spaces below the camera port. The assistant port is triangulated between arm two and the camera port for optimal access.

The robotic system is docked at the tip of the patient's left shoulder, aligned with the long axis of the operating table. A 30-degree down-angle camera is used to provide a view of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the esophagectomy using a robotic system, with a hook instrument in arm one and a frenzied bipolar in arm two, while utilizing a thirty-degree down camera for visualization.

The surgeon performs infrasigous dissection of the esophagus, beginning with an incision of the inferior pulmonary ligament. This incision allows the lung to fall away, facilitating the dissection as the surgeon separates the lung from the esophagus.

The surgeon resects the lower thoracic paraesophageal lymph nodes, removing them from the surgical field. Subsequently, the supradiaphragmatic lymph nodes at station III are excised. The dissection continues caudally.
The surgeon resects the lower thoracic paraesophageal lymph nodes along the esophagus, followed by continued dissection of the esophagus and pericardium.
The surgeon uses sustained traction and counter traction to meticulously dissect the esophagus or pericardium, with visible separation of tissue layers.

The surgeon identifies and clips any lung injury to prevent postoperative air leaks. The assistant provides sustained traction while the operating surgeon uses counter traction to continue the dissection of the esophagus.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the esophagus along its lower border using the 'hanging from rooftop' technique, which suspends the esophagus from above to facilitate inferior dissection.
The surgeon meticulously dissects over the pericardium, where the disease was identified in this patient, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon continues the inferior dissection of the esophagus, revealing the opposite lung. The dissection progresses distally until the right crest of the diaphragm is visualized.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the esophagus up to the gastroesophageal junction, visualizing and dissecting both diaphragmatic crura. The esophagus is incised superiorly after completing the inferior border dissection, allowing clear identification of the thoracic duct to prevent injury. The opposite lung is visible, and the left pleura is clearly seen. Due to the proximity of the disease, a patch of the opposite pleura is taken as an additional margin.

The surgeon clips any observed lung injury to prevent postoperative air leaks. The opposite pleura is opened, and intercostal drainage is placed on the opposite lung. An additional patch of pleura from the opposite side is taken for margin.

The surgeon performs a systematic dissection of the middle thoracic parasophageal lymph node station, focusing on the prominent subcarinal nodes. The dissection occurs over the pericardium and below the bifurcation of the trachea, with the pulmonary artery visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the esophagus along the azygos vein, resecting lymph nodes adjacent to the thoracic duct. The thoracic duct is visible with a circular appearance, and the azygos vein is also seen. Dissection reveals the bronchial artery originating from the intercostal arteries.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the azygous vein, ensuring preservation of the bronchial artery. This meticulous technique is crucial for maintaining bronchial artery integrity, which aids in preventing bronchorrhea and supports postoperative bronchial reflex and vascularization.

The surgeon completes the infrazygous dissection and proceeds to the suprazygous dissection. Important structures such as the superior vena cava, arch of the aorta, membranous trachea, subclavian artery, and impressions of the right and left vagus nerves are clearly visualized. Dissection is performed along the superior border of the esophagus, just above the membranous trachea.

The surgeon performs careful dissection over the membranous trachea using controlled traction and counter traction to avoid iatrogenic injury. The upper thoracic parasympathetic lymph node station 105 is resected. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is identified, and nodes along it are cleared, with the membranous trachea clearly visualized throughout the procedure.

The assistant depresses the membranous trachea using gauze to prevent iatrogenic injury during dissection. The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and the group of lymph nodes at station 106L, ensuring the delicate membranous trachea remains unharmed.

The surgeon continues cranial dissection of the esophagus, lifting it out with sustained traction and counter traction. The esophagus is fully mobilized, and the right recurrent laryngeal nerve is identified. Lymph nodes along the right recurrent laryngeal nerve are carefully dissected and removed.

The surgeon performs careful dissection of lymph nodes along the recurrent laryngeal nerve, using magnification to prevent injury to the nerve. The bronchial artery is preserved and traced to its origin from the intercostal artery. The azygous vein is filled and subsequently cut, facilitating the mobilization of the esophagus and preparation for stomach tube placement.

The surgeon preserves the bronchial artery during the esophagectomy, ensuring its integrity for postoperative recovery and preventing bronchorrhea. The trachea and its branches are identified, with the proprioceptive branch of the vagus nerve to the right main bronchus being preserved to maintain bronchial reflex. The vagus nerve is cut after the proprioceptive branch is given, and lymph nodes along the area are cleared.

The surgeon performs a complete dissection of the subcarinal group of lymph nodes, which are prominently visible. The dissection over the pericardium is shown, highlighting the proximity of the disease to this area. The pulmonary vein is clearly visible during the procedure.

Following the completion of the esophagectomy, the surgeon administers an intercostal nerve block by injecting 2-3 ml of 2% lignocaine into each intercostal space. Intercostal drainage tubes are then placed, with one positioned at the base and another at the apex of the thoracic cavity.
